target.title: [codex] Cron: repair empty sanitized final replies
target.body:
﻿???## Summary

- Problem: cron-owned agent runs could finish with no deliverable final text after sanitization and end up as silent `not-delivered` outcomes.
- Why it matters: scheduled reports can vanish without a clear error even though the cron job itself ran, which makes delivery failures hard to diagnose.
- What changed: add one repair pass for empty sanitized output, fail loudly when no deliverable text remains, enforce tool-disabled repair for embedded runs, and use an explicit CLI repair mode that resumes the first CLI session while disabling bundled MCP wiring.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not relax reasoning-tag sanitization, does not change normal non-cron agent turns, and does not address the legacy local WhatsApp health-check cron issue tracked separately in #60204.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Exact bug issue: none existed at PR open time
- Related #60204 for the misleading legacy WhatsApp health-check path uncovered during the same investigation
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: cron delivery could receive an empty final text after sanitization (for example when malformed reasoning tags consumed the visible reply), but the cron runner had no recovery pass and the delivery path did not convert that condition into a hard error.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the runner did not retry with a constrained no-tools finalization prompt, and the direct-delivery path treated missing synthesized text too softly.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the existing sanitization behavior is intentional; the gap was in cron finalization and delivery handling, not in the sanitizer itself.
- Why this regressed now: stricter sanitization can legitimately collapse malformed final replies to empty text, but cron still assumed a deliverable text reply would exist.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: ruled out transport-only failure for the investigated case; the actionable gap here was the empty-output cron path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts`, `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when cron final output becomes empty after sanitization, the runner performs one repair pass; embedded runs enforce `disableTools`, CLI runs resume the first CLI session and disable bundled MCP wiring, external-hook CLI cron runs keep `senderIsOwner=false` for both normal and repair calls, and if repair still yields no deliverable text the run fails loudly instead of silently ending `not-delivered`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in cron run finalization plus delivery dispatch, and the narrowest stable guardrail is to exercise those exact seams with mocked agent output.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing interim-retry coverage already covered acknowledgement retries but not empty sanitized output.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Cron jobs that lose their visible final text after sanitization now get one repair attempt before delivery.
- If no deliverable text exists after that repair attempt, the cron run records an explicit error instead of silently finishing as `not-delivered`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [delivery path sees no text] -> [silent not-delivered]

After:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [no-tools repair pass]
          -> [recovered text -> deliver] or [still empty -> explicit cron error]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the embedded repair path enforces `disableTools`; the CLI repair path is explicitly not represented as native-tool-free, resumes the first CLI session, and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring. The risk is limited to cron finalization behavior, and dedicated regression tests cover both embedded and CLI-backed paths.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node + pnpm checkout
- Model/provider: mocked embedded + CLI runner paths in targeted tests
- Integration/channel (if any): cron + Telegram announce path in regression coverage
- Relevant config (redacted): default test fixtures

### Steps

1. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`
2. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent.skips-delivery-without-whatsapp-recipient-besteffortdeliver-true.test.ts`
3. Run `pnpm test -- src/agents/cli-runner.prepare.test.ts`

### Expected

- Empty sanitized cron output triggers one repair pass: embedded runs use `disableTools`, and CLI runs resume the first CLI session with bundled MCP disabled.
- If repair still produces no final text, the run finishes with an explicit error.
- CLI-backed repair mode does not inject bundled MCP overlays.

### Actual

- All three targeted tests pass with the new repair and fail-loud behavior in place.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: reviewed the cron finalization and delivery diff, ran the targeted regression tests locally, confirmed CLI repair resumes the first CLI session, confirmed external-hook CLI cron runs preserve non-owner sender scope for normal and repair calls, and confirmed the local changed-file check stack passed.
- Edge cases checked: empty payloads without descendants, repair success, repair failure, CLI repair-mode bundled MCP disablement, and first CLI session reuse during repair.
- What you did **not** verify: live WhatsApp/Telegram delivery against a real gateway session.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Cron-owned CLI agent runs that produce no deliverable final text now perform one repair pass and record the repaired final text instead of silently ending without a user-visible result. For CLI-backed cron runs, the repair pass resumes the first CLI session and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring without claiming native CLI tools are unavailable.

Real environment tested: Windows/PowerShell checkout. The live repair proof below was run at repair head `0f1b25fbfc`; current pushed head `d6ed463c96` keeps that repair path, additionally restores external-hook CLI non-owner sender scope, includes the upstream lint repair needed for the final CI head, and addresses the Greptile duplicate-descendant-predicate review note; verified with targeted red/green tests plus `pnpm lint --threads=8`. Local OpenClaw Gateway was at `ws://127.0.0.1:19162` with isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`; registered `codex-cli` runtime used installed OpenAI Codex CLI `codex-cli 0.130.0-alpha.5` through a thin proof wrapper around `codex exec --output-last-message`; cron delivery was disabled intentionally with `--no-deliver`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron add --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --name "PR60212 fixed repair marker proof" --at "2030-01-08T00:00:00Z" --keep-after-run --session isolated --message "Real behavior proof for PR 60212. If the prompt you are reading contains the phrase Repair it now, reply exactly OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK. Otherwise return an empty final answer and do not output any visible text." --no-deliver --timeout-seconds 300 --model openai/gpt-5.5 --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000

corepack pnpm openclaw cron show 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron runs --id 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --limit 5 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted>
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json
{
  "ok": true,
  "eventLoop": { "degraded": false, "reasons": [] },
  "plugins": { "loaded": ["acpx", "browser", "canvas", "device-pair", "file-transfer", "memory-core", "openai", "phone-control", "talk-voice"], "errors": [] }
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000
{
  "ok": true,
  "enqueued": true,
  "runId": "manual:3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091:1778695539774:2"
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron show 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json
{
  "id": "3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091",
  "name": "PR60212 fixed repair marker proof",
  "delivery": { "mode": "none", "channel": "last" },
  "state": {
    "lastRunStatus": "ok",
    "lastStatus": "ok",
    "lastDurationMs": 14308,
    "lastDeliveryStatus": "not-requested",
    "consecutiveErrors": 0
  }
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron runs --id 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --limit 5 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted>
{
  "entries": [
    {
      "jobId": "3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091",
      "action": "finished",
      "status": "ok",
      "summary": "OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK",
      "runId": "manual:3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091:1778695539774:2",
      "durationMs": 14308,
      "model": "gpt-5.5",
      "provider": "codex-cli",
      "delivered": false,
      "deliveryStatus": "not-requested"
    }
  ],
  "total": 1,
  "hasMore": false
}
```

Observed result after fix: The real local gateway accepted the cron job, ran it through the registered `codex-cli` backend, recovered `OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK` during the repair flow, and recorded the run as `status: "ok"`. Delivery was not requested by design because the proof command used `--no-deliver`; the current code keeps the CLI mode explicit, resumes the first CLI session for repair, and does not hit the earlier native-tool fail-closed error.

What was not tested: External WhatsApp/Telegram send was not exercised; delivery was disabled to keep the local proof isolated and non-invasive.
Current-head ownership, lint, and Greptile cleanup verification after commit `d6ed463c96`:

```text
# RED before the ownership fix:
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts
FAIL passes senderIsOwner=false to external-hook CLI cron runs
AssertionError: expected true to be false

node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts
FAIL preserves external-hook non-owner scope during CLI empty-output repair
AssertionError: expected true to be false

# GREEN after the ownership fix:
$env:OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS='30000'; node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       5 passed (5)

$env:OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS='30000'; node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       9 passed (9)

$env:OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS='30000'; node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts

corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/agents/cli-runner/types.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.

$ node scripts/check-changed.mjs

git diff --check

```

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a cron run with intentionally empty final output now incurs one extra model pass before failing.
  - Mitigation: the retry is narrowly gated to empty, non-fatal, non-structured, no-descendant outcomes; embedded repair is tool-disabled and CLI repair is explicitly limited to bundled MCP suppression while resuming the first CLI session.
- Risk: CLI repair mode could accidentally preserve bundled MCP wiring.
  - Mitigation: `disableTools` now disables bundled MCP preparation, covered by `src/agents/cli-runner.prepare.test.ts`.

target.title: fix/agents-primary-model-selection-and-catalog-loading-41344
target.body: ## Summary - Fixes incorrect Primary Model selection when switching between agents in Agents > Overview. - Fixes partial / blank Primary Model dropdowns after refresh or first load by ensuring the model catalog is available on Agents Overview paths. - Adds targeted tests for both selection stability and catalog hydration behavior. - Keeps the fix scoped to the Agents Overview model-selection path. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #41344 - Related #46275 - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: 1. The agent details subtree could reuse stale DOM/select state when switching agents, which caused the Primary Model select to drift toward the previously viewed agent's state. 2. Agents Overview depended on a shared model catalog that was not guaranteed to be loaded on refresh / first-load / overview-only paths, so the select could render before the catalog was ready and degrade into a partial or blank-looking list. - Missing detection / guardrail: - Existing coverage covered some selected-value behavior, but it did not cover model-catalog hydration timing or the refresh/overview path where the catalog starts empty and arrives later. - Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): - Prior work around #41344 / #46275 addressed the agent-switching selection issue. Revalidating that fix on latest `upstream/main` exposed a second, related lifecycle issue in Agents Overview model-catalog loading. - Why this regressed now: - The Overview UI depends on model-catalog data, but not all entry paths guaranteed that the catalog was loaded before rendering. - If unknown, what was ruled out: - Ruled out a pure selected-state-only explanation. The partial/blank dropdown after refresh was caused by catalog availability/lifecycle, not just DOM selection drift. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should have caught this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `ui/src/ui/views/agents.browser.test.ts` - `ui/src/ui/views/agents.test.ts` - `ui/src/ui/controllers/models.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: - Switching between agents keeps the correct explicit Primary Model selected. - The inherit-default option text does not go blank. - When the model catalog is initially empty, Agents Overview shows a loading state instead of a misleading partial list. - When the catalog hydrates later, the full list appears without losing the selected value. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: - The bug spans both UI render behavior and shared model-catalog lifecycle, so a combination of focused controller/unit tests plus browser-level select behavior tests is the smallest reliable coverage. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): - Existing browser coverage covered some selection behavior, but not the catalog hydration path. - If no new test is added, why not: - N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - Switching between agents no longer causes the Primary Model select to show the wrong agent's value. - On refresh / first entry to Agents Overview, the Primary Model select no longer degrades into a misleading partial list. - While the model catalog is still loading, the UI now shows a loading state instead of pretending only a small subset of options exists. - Once the catalog loads, the full option list appears without losing the current selection. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: - N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Windows 10 - Runtime/container: local dev environment - Model/provider: local UI validation only - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): standard Agents UI configuration with default and non-default agents ### Steps 1. Open Agents > Overview > Model Selection > Primary Model. 2. Switch between a default agent and a non-default agent. 3. Refresh the page and reopen the Primary Model select immediately. 4. Optionally wait for model-catalog hydration or switch away/back to Overview. ### Expected - The select reflects the correct explicit value for the current agent. - The inherit-default option text is stable. - Refresh / first-load does not show a misleading partial catalog. - Once the catalog is available, the full model list appears without losing the selected value. ### Actual - Before this fix, switching agents could retain stale select state from another agent. - Before this fix, refresh / first-load could show only fallback options (for example inherit/current) or appear blank until the catalog became available later. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ### UI Evidence #### Before After refresh / first load, the Primary Model dropdown could show only fallback entries or appear blank. #### After After the fix, the Primary Model dropdown restores the full option list correctly after refresh / first load, without losing the selected value. ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Switched repeatedly between default and non-default agents and confirmed Primary Model stays tied to the current agent. - Refreshed the page and reopened Agents Overview to verify the select no longer falls into a misleading partial/blank state. - Confirmed catalog hydration restores the full list without losing the selected value. - Edge cases checked: - Inherit-default text path - Explicit non-default model selection - Refresh / first-load / return-to-overview behavior - What you did **not** verify: - Full end-to-end coverage across unrelated panels/modules - Additional stale/error UX beyond this fix's loading-state behavior ## AI Assistance - This PR was developed with AI assistance (CodeX). - I reviewed the resulting changes, reproduced the bug locally, and verified the final behavior manually. - Testing level: fully tested locally with targeted automated tests and manual UI verification. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes - Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No - Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No - If yes, exact upgrade steps: - N/A ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: - Revert this PR's two commits. - Files/config to restore: - `ui/src/ui/views/agents.ts` - `ui/src/ui/views/agents-panels-overview.ts` - `ui/src/ui/controllers/models.ts` - `ui/src/ui/app-render.ts` - `ui/src/ui/app-settings.ts` - `ui/src/ui/app-chat.ts` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: - Primary Model shows another agent's value after switching agents - Refresh / first-load shows only partial model options or a blank-looking select - Catalog hydration changes the list but drops the selected value ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: - Multiple UI entry points now ensure model-catalog availability, which could increase repeated refresh attempts if not properly deduped. - Mitigation: - The loading/ensure logic is centralized in the shared model controller and covered by targeted tests. - Risk: - Keyed subtree remounting can discard unsaved local UI state when switching agents. - Mitigation: - That tradeoff is intentional here because correctness of agent-specific model selection is more important than preserving stale subtree state across agent switches.

target.title: Add per-agent sandbox session visibility override feature
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: sandboxed session tools always applied the default sandbox session visibility clamp before considering the requesting agent, so `agents.list[].sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility` could be configured but was not consistently honored.
- Runtime impact: operators could not safely give one named sandboxed agent full configured session visibility while keeping other sandboxed agents clamped to spawned sessions.
- Change: session visibility resolution now derives the requester agent first, checks that agent's sandbox override, and falls back to `agents.defaults.sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility` only when no per-agent override exists.
- Compatibility: existing configs keep the same default `"spawned"` clamp unless a named agent explicitly sets `sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility`.

## User-Visible Behavior

New optional per-agent config:

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "sandbox": { "sessionToolsVisibility": "spawned" }
    },
    "list": [
      { "id": "tony", "sandbox": { "sessionToolsVisibility": "all" } }
    ]
  }
}
```

For sandboxed session tools, precedence is:

```text
agents.list[].sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility
agents.defaults.sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility
default spawned clamp
```

## Security Impact

- Data access scope can broaden only for an explicitly named sandboxed agent.
- The global/default sandbox behavior remains clamped to spawned sessions.
- Existing `tools.sessions.visibility` and `tools.agentToAgent` policy checks still apply; this does not bypass cross-agent policy.
- No secrets, credentials, network calls, shell execution, or gateway auth behavior changed.

## Files in This PR

- `CHANGELOG.md` - records the user-facing runtime/config behavior change.
- `docs/gateway/config-agents.md` - documents per-agent sandbox session visibility.
- `docs/gateway/config-tools.md` - documents session tool visibility and sandbox override precedence.
- `docs/tools/multi-agent-sandbox-tools.md` - documents multi-agent sandbox session tool behavior.
- `src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts` - wires requester identity into session tool construction.
- `src/agents/tools/session-status-tool.ts` - applies requester-aware sandbox visibility to `session_status`.
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-access.ts` - centralizes requester-aware sandbox visibility resolution.
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-helpers.ts` - forwards requester context through shared session helper wiring.
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-history-tool.ts` - applies requester-aware visibility to `sessions_history`.
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-list-tool.ts` - applies requester-aware visibility to `sessions_list`.
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.ts` - applies requester-aware visibility to `sessions_send`.
- `src/agents/openclaw-tools.session-status.test.ts` - covers requester-aware `session_status` behavior.
- `src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions-visibility.test.ts` - covers tool-factory wiring for the override.
- `src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts` - covers end-to-end session tool behavior through the OpenClaw tool factory.
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-access.test.ts` - covers access-layer override/fallback resolution.

## Verification

- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/agents/openclaw-tools.session-status.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions-visibility.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts src/agents/tools/session-status-tool.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-access.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-access.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-helpers.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-history-tool.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-list-tool.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=360000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/tools/sessions-access.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions-visibility.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.session-status.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions.test.ts --reporter=dot`
  - Result: 10 test files passed, 268 tests passed.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: a sandboxed named agent with `agents.list[].sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility="all"` can use the configured `tools.sessions.visibility` scope, while sandboxed agents without that override remain clamped to spawned sessions.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout using production `createOpenClawTools`, embedded OpenClaw gateway session handlers, and real temporary `sessions.json` stores for `main` and `tony`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx --input-type=module -e <inline proof script>` from the repo root. The script created an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, wrote real per-agent session stores, enabled embedded mode, constructed sandboxed `sessions_list` tools for `main` and `tony`, and compared observed session keys with expected visibility.

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "config": {
    "toolsSessionsVisibility": "all",
    "agentToAgent": { "enabled": true, "allow": ["*"] },
    "defaultSandboxSessionToolsVisibility": "spawned",
    "tonySandboxSessionToolsVisibility": "all"
  },
  "observed": {
    "mainSandboxedKeys": ["agent:main:subagent:main-child"],
    "tonySandboxedKeys": [
      "agent:main:main",
      "agent:main:subagent:main-child",
      "agent:tony:main",
      "agent:tony:subagent:tony-child"
    ]
  },
  "result": "PASS"
}
```

Observed result after fix: `main` stayed clamped to spawned sessions through the default sandbox setting; `tony` honored the per-agent `"all"` override and saw the full configured session scope when `tools.agentToAgent` allowed cross-agent access.

What was not tested: remote Crabbox/Testbox behavior. Crabbox is not installed in this shell, and the available Blacksmith CLI is unauthenticated.

## Risk and Rollback

- Risk: an operator may grant broad session visibility to the wrong named agent.
- Mitigation: the override is explicit per agent, default behavior is unchanged, and cross-agent policy remains enforced.
- Rollback: remove `agents.list[].sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility` from the agent entry to return to the default sandbox clamp.

target.title: feat(slack): add ignoreOtherMentions channel config
target.body:
## Summary

- Add `ignoreOtherMentions` option for Slack channel config, mirroring the existing Discord guild/channel feature
- When enabled, messages that mention another user (`<@U...>`) but do not explicitly mention the bot are dropped — even inside bot-participated threads
- This prevents the bot from replying when a user is talking to a coworker in a thread where the bot previously responded

## Slack vs Discord difference

Discord's `ignoreOtherMentions` checks `!wasMentioned && !implicitMention`. The Slack version intentionally omits the `!implicitMention` check because Slack's implicit mention (via `hasSlackThreadParticipation`) fires for **every** message in any bot-participated thread, which would make the feature ineffective.

## Config example

```json5
{
  channels: {
    slack: {
      channels: {
        "*": { ignoreOtherMentions: true },
      },
    },
  },
}
```

## Changed files

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/types.slack.ts` | Add `ignoreOtherMentions` to `SlackChannelConfig` |
| `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts` | Add zod validation for the new field |
| `extensions/slack/src/monitor/channel-config.ts` | Wire through type + resolver |
| `extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.ts` | Drop logic before mention gating |

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm build` passes
- [x] `pnpm test -- extensions/slack/src/monitor` — 236 tests pass
- [x] Manual test on live Slack workspace: bot no longer replies when tagging a coworker in a bot thread
- [x] Bot still replies when explicitly @mentioned in the same thread

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: cron: reject webhook URLs with embedded credentials
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: Cron webhook URLs could include embedded credentials (e.g. https://user:pass@example.com/hook), which can leak secrets in logs, config, or URLs. - Why it matters: URL-embedded credentials are discouraged and can expose secrets; using query params or headers is preferred. - What changed: normalizeHttpWebhookUrl now rejects URLs with username or password in the parsed URL. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to auth headers, query params, or other credential handling; only http(s)://user:pass@host URLs are rejected. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Cron webhook URLs with embedded credentials (e.g. https://user:secret@host/path) are now rejected as invalid. URLs without credentials continue to work. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`) - New/changed network calls? (`No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? (`No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (darwin) - Runtime/container: Node 22+ - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): CRON webhook delivery - Relevant config (redacted): N/A ### Steps 1. Add or update a cron job with webhook delivery using a URL with embedded credentials, e.g. https://user:pass@example.invalid/webhook. 2. Observe that the webhook URL is rejected (job will not use it). 3. Use the same job with https://example.invalid/webhook (no credentials) and confirm it is accepted. ### Expected - URLs with user:pass@ in the authority are rejected. - URLs without embedded credentials continue to work. ### Actual - Behavior matches above ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: normalizeHttpWebhookUrl returns null for URLs like http://user:pass@host/path; returns the URL for http://host/path. - Edge cases checked: URLs with only username (user@host), only password (not typical in URLs), and no credentials. - What you did **not** verify: End-to-end cron job creation via CLI or UI with credential URLs; live webhook delivery. ## Review Conversations - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. target.title: feat(context-engine): add interceptCompaction contract for context-engine plugins
target.body:
TLDR - lets other context systems turn off/replace codex compaction (when it calls to compact it uses them versus its native).

## Summary

Adds an optional `interceptCompaction(request)` method to the `ContextEngine` plugin contract so engines can replace the runtime's default `session_before_compact` GPT-driven summarization with their own assembly — without leaving the runtime's compaction lifecycle.

Motivating use case: [lossless-claw](https://github.com/Martian-Engineering/lossless-claw/pull/665) assembles a lossless raw + summary pyramid on disk and wants to swap in its assembled context whenever pi-coding-agent would otherwise trigger codex's GPT compaction. With the contract added here, lossless-claw can keep its plugin scope narrow (one new method on the engine) instead of forking or monkey-patching the runtime.

## Architecture — two compaction lanes, two capability flags

OpenClaw + pi-coding-agent expose two distinct compaction flows that this PR makes explicit:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     OpenClaw queued-compaction lane                       │
│              ┌──────────────┐   afterTurn /        │
│              │ engine plugin│ ◄─/compact / explicit│
│              │.compact(…)   │   queue              │
│              └──────────────┘                      │
│   Gate: info.ownsCompaction === true                                      │
│   Where: src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.queued.ts                  │
│   Engine: full control of timing, target, assembly                        │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│             pi-coding-agent SDK session_before_compact lane               │
│                                                                           │
│  codex hits 90%      ┌─────────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────────┐ │
│  context (auto-      │ session_before_     │ ─► │ compactionInterceptExt│ │
│  compact threshold)  │ compact emit        │    │ (this PR)             │ │
│                      └─────────────────────┘    └──────────┬───────────┘ │
│                                                            ▼               │
│                                              engine.interceptCompaction()  │
│                                                  │                         │
│                                  handled: true  ─┴─►  use {summary}        │
│                                  handled: false ────►  fall through to     │
│                                                       safeguard/codex GPT  │
│                                                                           │
│  Gate: info.interceptsCompaction === true                                 │
│  Where: src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-intercept.{ts,-runtime.ts}         │
│  Engine: replacement summary only; trigger is owned by the SDK           │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Engines may declare BOTH flags (lossless-claw does): codex still fires `session_before_compact` on in-attempt overflow even when the engine owns the openclaw queued lane.

## What's in this PR

| # | File | Change |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `src/context-engine/types.ts` | New `CompactionInterceptRequest` / `CompactionInterceptResult` types, new `info.interceptsCompaction?: boolean` flag, new optional `interceptCompaction?(...)` method on `ContextEngine`. Re-exported via plugin-sdk. |
| 2 | `src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-intercept-runtime.ts` (new) | Per-session runtime registry (WeakMap keyed by SessionManager identity) carrying the resolved engine + optional `sessionKey`. Modeled on `compaction-safeguard-runtime.ts`. |
| 3 | `src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-intercept.ts` (new) | The new `ExtensionFactory`. Defensive against: no runtime, no engine, engine without method, undefined sessionFile, thrown errors. Returns `{compaction: {...}}` on `handled:true` or `undefined` on fall-through. |
| 4 | `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/extensions.ts` | `buildEmbeddedExtensionFactories` accepts optional `activeContextEngine` + `sessionKey`. Pushes the new factory AFTER safeguard so SDK last-truthy-wins lets intercept override safeguard's fallback. Gate is `info.interceptsCompaction === true` only — no exclusion for `ownsCompaction`. |
| 5 | `src/agents/pi-settings.ts` | Two related changes: (a) New `engineOwnsPostCompactHeadroom` predicate. `applyPiCompactionSettingsFromConfig` gains optional `contextEngineInfo` and auto-zeroes `reserveTokensFloor` when `ownsCompaction \|\| interceptsCompaction` (without auto-zero, an engine like lossless-claw targeting ~65% post-compact headroom would have an additional 20K-token floor reserved on top). (b) `shouldDisablePiAutoCompaction` carves out an `interceptsCompaction === true` exception so Pi's threshold check stays enabled and the `session_before_compact` event still emits — without this the intercept extension is dead code. The silent-overflow branch deliberately ignores the exception (provider can truncate without warning regardless). |
| 6 | `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts` | Threads `activeContextEngine?.info` + `params.sessionKey` at both the initial prepared-settings build and the post-`resourceLoader.reload()` re-apply. |
| 7 | `src/agents/command/cli-compaction.ts` | Threads `contextEngineInfo` so the `/compact` CLI path matches. The test-injectable `CliCompactionDeps` shape gains the new field. |
| 8 | Inner compaction-LLM session (`pi-embedded-runner/compact.ts:992`) | Intentionally NOT threaded — that session has no user-facing engine; the existing comment block is expanded to cover both calls. |
| 9 | Tests | New `compaction-intercept.test.ts` (~18 cases). Extended `extensions.test.ts` (~10 cases). Extended `pi-settings.test.ts` (~8 cases covering both the auto-zero gate and the `shouldDisablePiAutoCompaction` exception). |

## Behavior changes

- **None by default.** The contract is opt-in: engines that don't declare `info.interceptsCompaction = true` see zero behavior change. The new ExtensionFactory is a no-op when no such engine is active.
- **For opt-in engines:** `session_before_compact` routes through `engine.interceptCompaction()`. On `handled:true`, the engine's summary replaces the codex GPT call. On `handled:false` or thrown errors, the runtime falls back to its default. The SDK short-circuits on `{cancel:true}` returns (auth-failure cases from safeguard) and intercept is not called in those cases — documented limitation.
- **Reserve-token floor auto-zero:** for engines declaring `interceptsCompaction` or `ownsCompaction`, Pi's `reserveTokensFloor` is treated as 0 because the engine takes responsibility for post-compaction headroom.
- **Pi auto-compaction stays on for intercepting engines:** when an engine declares `interceptsCompaction === true`, Pi's `_checkCompaction` is NOT force-disabled (it would normally be when `ownsCompaction === true` or `mode === "safeguard"`). The threshold check is the only emit point for `session_before_compact`; disabling it would silently turn the intercept extension into dead code. The silent-overflow-prone branch is intentionally exempt — that lane is a hard safety guarantee.

## Compatibility

- Plugin SDK is private (`packages/plugin-sdk/package.json` is `"private": true`), so no version bump. Third-party plugins importing the new types from `openclaw/plugin-sdk` see them automatically once this lands.
- New `ContextEngine.interceptCompaction?` method is **optional** — existing engines (legacy, custom plugins) don't need any changes. New `info.interceptsCompaction` flag is also optional.
- SDK `{cancel: true}` short-circuit limitation: when the safeguard returns cancel (auth/model failure), intercept is not called. This is the correct degradation for the auth case — intercept can't summarize without a model either — but is documented in `compaction-intercept.ts` so future readers understand the ordering tradeoff.

## Adversarial review

Four independent adversarial review waves were performed by separate research agents reading the source code from scratch. Findings addressed in order:

- **Wave A** (commit `058997508ab`): 1 P0 architecture-killer (gate exclusion blocking the only legitimate consumer), 3 P0 CI compile/lint failures, 3 P1 polish items.
- **Wave B** (commit `b97b9e32e45`): 1 P0 lint cascade (`!== true` / `=== false` against discriminated-union booleans, `unbound-method`, `curly`) and 3 P1 items: misleading `trigger` field always-returns, stale docstrings claiming "no-op for ownsCompaction" (contradicting wave-A gate change), missing handler-routing test for both-flags engines.
- **Wave 3** (commit `049d7d640a5`): 2 stale docstrings in `types.ts` and `extensions.ts` left over from the wave-A gate change.
- **Wave 4** (commit `fe7455a9d9e`): runtime smoke-test against lossless-claw discovered that the intercept extension was registered correctly but the underlying `session_before_compact` event never emitted. Root cause: `shouldDisablePiAutoCompaction` in `pi-settings.ts` was force-disabling Pi auto-compaction whenever `ownsCompaction === true` (and engines like lossless-claw declare both flags), so Pi's `shouldCompact()` short-circuited on `!settings.enabled` before the threshold was ever evaluated and the event never fired. Fix: carve out an exception so `interceptsCompaction === true` keeps Pi auto-compaction enabled. The silent-overflow branch remains gated only on `silentOverflowProneProvider` because that lane is a hard provider-truncation guarantee. New test cases cover the carve-out.

All findings addressed in dedicated commits with cross-references in commit messages.


## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: context-engine plugins that declare both `ownsCompaction: true` and `interceptsCompaction: true` must keep Pi auto-compaction enabled so Pi can emit `session_before_compact`, while still letting the plugin replace the default GPT-driven compaction summary through `engine.interceptCompaction(request)`.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw dev install with `/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw` symlinked into the PR-branch source tree, all PR commits applied, Gateway restarted through `launchctl bootout` / `launchctl bootstrap`, and the `lossless-claw` context-engine plugin loaded. `lossless-claw` is the both-flags engine this PR is for (`ownsCompaction: true`, `interceptsCompaction: true`; see Martian-Engineering/lossless-claw#665).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
grep -A4 '^function shouldDisablePiAutoCompaction' dist/pi-settings-BNY3tGnv.js
curl -s http://localhost:18789/healthz
ps -p "$(launchctl list | awk '/ai\\.openclaw\\.gateway$/ {print $1}')" -o pid,etime,command
tail -F ~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log | grep -E '(listening|lossless-claw)'
grep -c '"type":"session_before_compact"' ~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/<uuid>.trajectory.jsonl
pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/pi-settings.test.ts --pool=forks
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ grep -A4 '^function shouldDisablePiAutoCompaction' dist/pi-settings-BNY3tGnv.js
function shouldDisablePiAutoCompaction(params) {
        const intercepts = params.contextEngineInfo?.interceptsCompaction === true;
        return params.contextEngineInfo?.ownsCompaction === true && !intercepts
                || params.compactionMode === "safeguard" && !intercepts
                || params.silentOverflowProneProvider === true;
}

$ curl -s http://localhost:18789/healthz
{"ok":true,"status":"live"}

$ ps -p $(launchctl list | awk '/ai\.openclaw\.gateway$/ {print $1}') -o pid,etime,command
75484   04:41 /opt/homebrew/opt/node/bin/node /opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/index.js gateway --port 18789

$ tail -F ~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log | grep -E '(listening|lossless-claw)'
2026-05-14T00:01:10.403+07:00 [gateway] http server listening
  (7 plugins: acpx, browser, codex, cortex, gbrain, lossless-claw, telegram; 10.9s)
2026-05-14T00:01:12.770+07:00 [plugins] loading lossless-claw
  from /Volumes/LEXAR/repos/lossless-claw/dist/index.js

$ grep -c '"type":"session_before_compact"' ~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/<uuid>.trajectory.jsonl
0

$ pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/pi-settings.test.ts --pool=forks
 ✓ |agents-core|    pi-settings.test.ts (43 tests) 40ms
 ✓ |agents-support| pi-settings.test.ts (43 tests) 27ms

  Test Files  2 passed (2)
  Tests       86 passed (86)
```

Observed result after fix: the compiled Gateway bundle contains the new `interceptsCompaction` exception, Gateway boots successfully with `lossless-claw` loaded, and the table-driven runtime tests pass for the exact both-flags case that previously disabled Pi auto-compaction. Before this fix, the same live setup ran for 24 hours of Eva-channel usage with the intercept extension registered but unreachable: 0 `compaction-intercept` log lines, 0 `session_before_compact` events in the 27.5 MB trajectory, 0 rows in lossless-claw's `compaction_events` SQLite table, and 0 `Generating new conversation summary` markers in codex rollout files. The root cause was deterministic: `shouldDisablePiAutoCompaction({ ownsCompaction: true })` returned `true`, `setCompactionEnabled(false)` ran, and Pi short-circuited before evaluating the threshold that emits `session_before_compact`.

What was not tested: the next live high-context Eva turn has not yet crossed the 90% `openai-codex/gpt-5.5` threshold after the fix, so the final wild-fire `compaction-intercept` Gateway log line is still pending. A monitor is tailing `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log`; this PR will be updated when that event emits.

target.title: fix(agents): reject invalid process.action at the tool invocation boundary
target.body:
## Summary

- `createProcessTool().execute` in `src/agents/bash-tools.process.ts` casts raw tool args as the typed action union without first validating that `action` is one of the documented string values. When a model emits `process({action: {}})` (the GLM/Z.AI contamination pattern reported in #69582), `params.action` is the literal `{}` object — `params.action === "list"` is false, every later branch checks `!params.sessionId` first, and the agent receives the misleading `sessionId is required for this action.` error. The retry path then re-sends the same malformed payload, producing the infinite loop the reporter described.
- Validate `action` as the first runtime guard inside `execute`: extract `rawAction` as `unknown` directly from `args`, compare against a new module-level `PROCESS_TOOL_ACTIONS` set (kept in sync with the schema description in `bash-tools.schemas.ts:53`), and on miss return an explicit `Invalid 'action' parameter ... Do not retry with the same argument shape.` failure with the standard `details: { status: "failed" }` shape.
- Add five `process tool action validation` cases in `src/agents/bash-tools.test.ts` covering the shapes the reporter and clawsweeper called out (`{}`, empty string, `null`, number, array, unknown action string) plus a positive control that every documented action string passes the new guard.

## Related Issues

Closes #69582.

## Type of Change

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] New feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Documentation
- [ ] Other (describe below)

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Reporter `ZKANGGIT` (#69582, 2026-04-21) ran OpenClaw 2026.3.24 with local/glm-5 and saw `process({action: {}})` cause an infinite retry loop with no actionable feedback. ClawSweeper's source-level review on the issue confirmed: `processSchema` declares `action` as a plain `Type.String` so the schema surface cannot reject a wrong type, and `createProcessTool` casts raw args to the typed union and falls through to the generic missing-`sessionId` path for `action: {}`. Recommended action: *"Add narrow schema-aware validation for invalid `process.action` type/value at the tool invocation boundary."* After this patch, the same malformed call returns a shape-aware error naming the rejected `action` value and instructing the model not to retry with the same shape, so the loop terminates.

**Real environment tested**: Local Linux checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` at `upstream/main` `178a50e017`, branched into `fix/process-tool-validate-action-type-69582`. Source-level walk against the bot's reproduction recipe: bash-tools.process.ts uses `const params = args as { action: "list" | "poll" | ... }` (line 246 on patched branch / line 240 on `upstream/main`) without runtime validation; the first real-side-effect branch is `if (params.action === "list")` followed by `if (!params.sessionId)`. After the patch, `rawAction = args.action as unknown` is checked against `PROCESS_TOOL_ACTIONS` before any of those branches.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

I invoked the patched `createProcessTool` directly through the same `node_modules` the production agent runtime uses, exercising the exact malformed payload reported in #69582. The capture below is the actual return value the agent's tool-call dispatcher receives at runtime — the agent loop reads `result.content[0].text` straight off this object and feeds it back to the model. Both the pre-patch and the post-patch run use the same invocation script; the only change between them is whether `src/agents/bash-tools.process.ts` is `upstream/main` or this PR's branch.

```ts
// repro-69582.mjs — direct runtime invocation of the live tool
import { createProcessTool } from "<repo>/src/agents/bash-tools.process.ts";
const tool = createProcessTool();
const r1 = await tool.execute("call-1", { action: {}, sessionId: "whatever" });
console.log("action:{} ->", r1.content?.[0]?.text);
const r2 = await tool.execute("call-2", { action: null });
console.log("action:null ->", r2.content?.[0]?.text);
const r3 = await tool.execute("call-3", { action: "delete" });
console.log("action:'delete' ->", r3.content?.[0]?.text);
const r4 = await tool.execute("call-4", { action: "list" });
console.log("action:'list' ->", r4.content?.[0]?.text?.slice(0, 80));
```

Output captured 2026-05-12 on Node v22.22.2:

**Pre-patch** (`upstream/main` `178a50e017`, no PR applied):
```
action:{} -> Unknown action [object Object]
action:null -> sessionId is required for this action.
action:'delete' -> sessionId is required for this action.
action:'list' -> No running or recent sessions.
```

**Post-patch** (this PR branch `f514d9a9ad` applied to the same `upstream/main`):
```
action:{} -> Invalid 'action' parameter for process tool (got object: {}). Expected one of: list, poll, log, write, send-keys, submit, paste, kill, clear, remove. Do not retry with the same argument shape.
action:null -> Invalid 'action' parameter for process tool (got object: null). Expected one of: list, poll, log, write, send-keys, submit, paste, kill, clear, remove. Do not retry with the same argument shape.
action:'delete' -> Invalid 'action' parameter for process tool (got string: "delete"). Expected one of: list, poll, log, write, send-keys, submit, paste, kill, clear, remove. Do not retry with the same argument shape.
action:'list' -> No running or recent sessions.
```

Two behaviour deltas the runtime observably emits:

1. `action: null` and `action: "delete"` no longer return the misleading `sessionId is required for this action.` — exactly the message the reporter quoted as fuelling the infinite retry loop. Every invalid shape now returns the same shape-aware error explicitly telling the agent's retry path not to resubmit.
2. The error message names the *received* value (`object: {}`, `object: null`, `string: "delete"`) so the agent and downstream logs can distinguish "malformed JSON object" from "unknown action string" without parsing the message.

The `action: "list"` line is identical in both runs (`No running or recent sessions.`), proving the new guard short-circuits only on invalid shapes and never touches the documented action paths.

**Evidence after fix**:

- `src/agents/bash-tools.process.ts`: the new guard runs first inside `execute`. For `args = { action: {}, sessionId: "anything" }`, `rawAction` is the empty object, `typeof rawAction !== "string"` returns true, and the guard returns the `Invalid 'action' parameter for process tool (got object: {}). Expected one of: list, poll, log, write, send-keys, submit, paste, kill, clear, remove. Do not retry with the same argument shape.` failure before any `sessionId is required` codepath can fire.
- For `args = { action: "delete", sessionId: "anything" }`, `rawAction` is the string `"delete"`, `PROCESS_TOOL_ACTIONS.has("delete")` returns false, and the guard returns the same `Invalid 'action' parameter` failure naming the bad value.
- For `args = { action: "list" }`, the new guard passes (because `"list" ∈ PROCESS_TOOL_ACTIONS`) and the existing list-action branch handles it; the test asserts this against every documented action.
- `src/agents/bash-tools.test.ts` adds one new `describe` block (`process tool action validation (#69582)`) with four cases pinning the contract: malformed object, broader non-string shapes (`""`, `null`, `42`, `["list"]`), unknown action string, and the positive control over the 10 documented actions. Each case asserts the absence of `sessionId is required` in the response text, so a future refactor cannot re-introduce the misleading message ordering.

**Observed result after fix**: A subsequent `process({action: {}})` invocation returns `{content: [{type: "text", text: "Invalid 'action' parameter for process tool (got object: {}). Expected one of: list, poll, log, write, send-keys, submit, paste, kill, clear, remove. Do not retry with the same argument shape."}], details: {status: "failed"}}`. The agent's retry path now sees a shape-aware refusal and the loop terminates. Behaviour for valid `action` values is unchanged — every existing test case in `bash-tools.test.ts`, `bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts`, and `bash-tools.process.input-hints.test.ts` continues to use canonical action strings that the new guard accepts.

**What was not tested**: I did not exercise GLM-specific argument repair or any provider-side malformed-payload path — clawsweeper's review explicitly flagged that as out-of-scope without a raw provider fixture, and the present fix deliberately stops at runtime validation at the tool boundary so it is safe for *every* provider that can emit a malformed action. The runtime invocation captured above runs the tool against the same `node_modules` CI's `checks-node-core-fast` lane uses.

## Checklist

- [x] No schema-shape change — `bash-tools.schemas.ts` `processSchema` keeps `action: Type.String(...)`. Promoting to a literal-union would also work but expands the wire contract more than needed and risks breaking existing well-typed providers; this PR stays at runtime validation, which is what clawsweeper's "best possible solution" recommended.
- [x] No CHANGELOG entry (per `AGENTS.md`: contributors do not edit it; maintainer adds at landing).
- [x] No new exports or public-API surfaces — `PROCESS_TOOL_ACTIONS` is module-local.
- [x] Targets `main`.

target.title: fix(acp): honor tagVisibility.agent_thought_chunk for thought-stream events
target.body:
## Summary

Honor the operator-tunable `tagVisibility.agent_thought_chunk` flag for ACP thought-stream `text_delta` events. Closes catalog finding #12.

## Bug detail

Wire probe captured 2 `agent_thought_chunk` sessionUpdates per turn from copilot's ACP runtime. The deployed openclaw config set `tagVisibility.agent_thought_chunk: true`, but the operator never saw the thoughts in any channel. The projector at `src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.ts:408-411` had a hard `event.stream !== "output"` guard that ran BEFORE the `isAcpTagVisible` check, so the operator-tunable visibility flag was structurally unreachable for thought-stream events.

## How to reproduce

Unit-level repro in this branch before the fix commit (`f0180a023c`):

```bash
git checkout f0180a023c
pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.thought-chunk-delivery.test.ts
# Expected: 'delivers thought chunks when tagVisibility.agent_thought_chunk=true' FAILS
```

After the fix commit (`379320c173`) the same test passes.

## Root cause

`src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.ts:408-411` (pre-fix):

```ts
if (event.type === \"text_delta\") {
  if (event.stream && event.stream !== \"output\") {
    return; // drops ALL non-\"output\" streams unconditionally, including \"thought\"
  }
  if (!isAcpTagVisible(settings, event.tag)) {
    return;
  }
}
```

The `acpx` translator faithfully tags the event with `stream: \"thought\"`; the projector unconditionally discarded it before consulting visibility settings.

## Fix approach

Reorder the guards: `isAcpTagVisible` runs FIRST and is authoritative; only if visibility is denied does the function return. The stream check is then narrowed so that \"output\" and \"thought\" streams both fall through to delivery, while other unknown streams (e.g., a hypothetical `stream: \"diagnostic\"`) still drop. Default behavior unchanged: `tagVisibility.agent_thought_chunk: false` (default) keeps thought events hidden.

## Tests

- **Red test (now GREEN):** `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.thought-chunk-delivery.test.ts` — 3 scenarios cover default-hidden, visibility-true (the fix), and output-stream control.
- **Adjacent suite:** `src/auto-reply/reply/` — 1538 tests pass / 0 fail (115 files).

## Catalog reference

Catalog finding: #12. Investigation lives in branch `edpiva/acp-native-session-investigation` (not yet upstream) at `docs/plans/2026-05-08-acp-findings-catalog.md`.

## Verification commands

\`\`\`bash
pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.thought-chunk-delivery.test.ts   # GREEN
pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/                                              # 1538 pass / 0 fail
\`\`\`

## Notes for reviewers

The new guard correctly drops unknown-stream events (e.g., `stream: \"diagnostic\"`) regardless of `tagVisibility`, but no test exercises that path explicitly. The behavior is the desired one — coverage gap only, not a wrong fix. Happy to add a fourth test case if you want it.

---

## Live behavior repro (deployed container)

**Container:** `codeclaw-openclaw:clean` running OpenClaw 2026.5.6 (`e9d4a0a`)
**PII note:** UUIDs are random; nothing to redact in this slice.

### Operator opted into thought delivery, copilot emitted thought events, projector dropped them all

Live config in the container:

```
$ docker exec codeclaw-openclaw jq '.acp.stream.tagVisibility' /home/codeclaw/.openclaw/openclaw.json
{
  "tool_call": true,
  "tool_call_update": true,
  "agent_thought_chunk": true,
  "plan": true
}
```

Operator has `agent_thought_chunk: true` set (i.e., wants thoughts delivered).

Copilot's `events.jsonl` for the relevant ACP session shows the events arrived on the wire:

```
$ docker exec codeclaw-openclaw grep -c agent_thought_chunk \
    /home/codeclaw/.copilot/session-state/882435ee-…/events.jsonl
2
```

Pre-fix, those events were silently dropped at `acp-projector.ts:408-411` because the unconditional `event.stream !== "output"` guard ran BEFORE `isAcpTagVisible` could consult the operator-tunable visibility flag. Operator never saw a thought chunk, even after explicitly opting in.

After this PR (fix `379320c173` + coverage `bdf2df35ab`), `isAcpTagVisible` runs first and is authoritative, then the stream allow-list narrows to `"output" | "thought"`. Hypothetical unknown streams (e.g., `"diagnostic"`) still drop regardless of `tagVisibility` — covered by the 4th test added in the cleanup commit.

target.title: Improve model call telemetry
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw model-call traces made the current-turn input easy to see, but the real prompt burden could be split across model input messages, system instructions, and tool definitions. Token usage also lived primarily on the separate run-level `openclaw.model.usage` span, which made individual model-call debugging harder in trace viewers.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds safe model-call prompt metadata to diagnostic events and OTel spans:

- `openclaw.model_call.prompt.*` for input message count/chars, system prompt chars, tool definition count/chars, and total prompt chars.
- `openclaw.model_call.usage.*` and `gen_ai.usage.*` on model-call spans when the provider result exposes per-call usage.

Raw prompt, system prompt, tool definition, and output content remain gated behind the existing `diagnostics.otel.captureContent` policy.

## User Impact

Operators can diagnose prompt pollution and prompt cost from the model-call span itself without enabling raw content capture. Existing aggregate run accounting stays on `openclaw.model.usage`, and trusted trace correlation remains unchanged.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.test.ts extensions/diagnostics-otel/src/service.test.ts`
- `node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/gateway/opentelemetry.md`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` with `AUTOREVIEW_AUTO_TESTS=0`: clean, no accepted/actionable findings

target.title: feat(claude): add claude-bridge app-server harness extension
target.body:
## Summary

TL;DR:
Inspiration: https://openclaw.ai/blog/openai-models-in-openclaw-done-right; goal: achieve parity for Claude

New feature; issue https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/93793

OpenClaw currently has no first-class harness for Anthropic models. Anthropic users either fall back to a generic provider client (no native tool execution, no extended-thinking awareness, no per-turn rate-limit surfacing) or run a Claude harness out-of-tree with no contract for OpenClaw's plugin-SDK capabilities and features.

The codex harness already ships in-tree with the same shape we want — `extensions/codex/src/app-server/` is a codex-app-server JSON-RPC client over stdio. We have a working Anthropic-side equivalent built and running in production on the author's setup; opening it for maintainer steer before doing more SDK work on top.

Harness: this PR
JSON-RPC server: https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge

**What is the intended outcome?**

- Bundled extension `@openclaw/claude` registers `claude-bridge` as a harness option in the model picker.
- At turn-start, the extension spawns an `openclaw-claude-bridge` subprocess and speaks the codex-shaped JSON-RPC protocol over stdio, directly wrapping the latest claude agent SDK. The server (currently published as `@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge`  https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge — fork-preview path; would migrate to `@openclaw/openclaw-claude-bridge` if the maintainers republish it) handles Anthropic API I/O, rate-limit surfacing, image-payload sanitization, and plugin-thread-config persistence.
- Bridge-side handles event projection into OpenClaw's notification/accumulator surface, transcript mirroring with idempotency keys, dynamic-tool aggregate output cap, vision-tools de-duplication, and `/claude` slash commands.
- See `extensions/claude/README.md` for the full feature map.

**What is intentionally out of scope?**

- Republishing the server under `@openclaw/openclaw-claude-bridge` — needs maintainer decision; the `@zeroaltitude` scope dependency is the current placeholder.
- Migrating to OpenClaw's standard plugin-SDK provider hooks beyond what `extensions/codex/` already uses. The bridge follows codex's pattern verbatim where applicable.
- Anything outside the harness bridge surface.

**What does success look like?** Anthropic users get parity with codex users: native tool dispatch, rate-limit-aware errors, extended-thinking-friendly transcript mirroring, harness-level event projection. No regressions to existing harnesses.

**What should reviewers focus on?**

1. **Scope concern — am I taking too much core?** The branch touches `src/plugins/command-registration.ts`, `src/plugins/current-plugin-metadata-snapshot.ts`, `src/plugins/agent-tool-result-middleware-types.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-native-slash-fast-path.ts`, `src/agents/model-runtime-aliases.ts`, and `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts`. Each is small and necessary to wire a harness extension, but the contribution guide says "one thing per PR." Happy to split into prep PRs first if maintainers prefer.
2. **External-plugin vs. bundled-extension** — Per CONTRIBUTING.md "most features should be third-party plugins." Open to packaging this entirely as an external plugin instead of `extensions/claude/`, if that is preferred.
3. **Anthropic-API behavior delegated to a separate server repo** — Most of the actual API logic lives in the server (https://github.com/zeroaltitude/openclaw-plugins/tree/main/openclaw-claude/server -- https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge). Reviewers will probably want to inspect that companion repo before approving.
4. **`claude-bridge` harness id naming** — there's no precedent for "<vendor>-bridge" harness names; renamable. (The reason I did this initially was to ensure that I wasn't representing that this work was by anthropic -- matters more on the server naming than the in-openclaw extension tho)

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Anthropic-model agents previously had no in-tree harness; the bridge unlocks native tool runs against Claude through the same JSON-RPC pattern codex uses.
- **Real environment tested:** Author's openclaw gateway running on Linux (WSL2 / Ubuntu), Node 22.x. Author's agents  are configured with `claude-bridge` as their harness and are in daily active use against `claude-sonnet-4-6` and `claude-opus-4-7`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. `pnpm install && pnpm build`
  2. `openclaw gateway restart --deep`
  3. Send a Slack DM to one of the claude-bridge agents
  4. Agent responds with native Anthropic tools used; transcript file contains the projected events.
  5. `/claude status` in the same thread prints shared-client liveness.
- **Evidence after fix (fresh capture, 2026-05-28):** This gateway runs the claude-bridge harness as the default runtime for all Anthropic models (`agents.defaults.models.*.agentRuntime.id = "claude-bridge"`), so the harness is exercised continuously across multiple agents. Captured live:
  1. **Bridge liveness** — shared-client lifecycle today: `claude-bridge: spawned ×5 / initialized ×5 / process exited ×5` (restarts and re-initializes without manual intervention).
  2. **A turn completing with visible tool/progress output** — one bridge turn dispatching consecutive tools, each completing, with per-tool provenance timing from the observability plugin: `ToolSearch → after_tool_call 1.9ms`, `exec → 1.7ms`, `Write → 1.9ms`, `Agent → 1.9ms`.
  3. **Transcript / result event stream** — the run persists a projected event stream to `~/.openclaw/agents/<id>/sessions/<uuid>.jsonl`: parentId-chained `message` events covering the user turn, assistant `tool_use` blocks, and `tool_result` blocks.
  4. **`/claude status`** (live, from a bridge-backed agent):
     ```
     Claude app-server status
     Shared client: running
     Command: openclaw-claude-bridge
     Last stderr: [turn-runner] system event {"subtype":"init"} / {"subtype":"status"} / unhandled SDK event {"type":"rate_limit_event"}
     ```
  *Log-hygiene note:* a few `claude-bridge: runAttempt failed` lines appear in the same logs; they are not bridge defects — one class is a context-overflow on a zero-message scheduled run (auto-compaction correctly skipped), the other is transient model-call failover during an MCP reconnect; both recovered on the next turn. Earlier daily-driver context still applies: Tabitha (claude-bridge) has been in active production use for ~2 weeks.
- **Observed result after fix:** Anthropic turns complete normally; tool calls fire; rate-limit messages are user-actionable; resume across restart works via the `.claude-binding.json` sidecar.
- **What was not tested:**
  - Windows/macOS gateway hosts (only Linux/WSL2 verified).
  - Bun runtime (Node 22 only).
  - Anthropic enterprise/Bedrock/Vertex API surfaces (only public Anthropic API).
  - Concurrent multi-account scenarios beyond 3 agents.
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** Author runs a single-host gateway. The server is currently published under the author's `@zeroaltitude` npm scope (fork-preview), so reproducing this requires installing the bridge from that scope until maintainers republish.
- **Before evidence:** N/A — no prior in-tree harness for Anthropic to compare against.

## Tests and validation

- Real world use for over a month on a broad diversity of tasks with observed, anecdotal, improvements in coding, token usage and well executed long running tasks
- `pnpm build` ✓ (local)
- `pnpm test extensions/claude` ✓ (local — extension unit tests, including event projector, dynamic-tools middleware port, thread fork policy envelope, transcript mirror dedupe)
- `pnpm test:extensions` not run since last rebase — recommend Testbox/Crabbox run before non-draft review.
- No `CHANGELOG.md` edit per contributor rule; maintainer to add on land.

## Risk checklist

- **Did user-visible behavior change?** Yes — new `claude-bridge` harness appears in model picker; new `/claude` slash command surface.
- **Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** Yes — new plugin `configSchema` (see `extensions/claude/openclaw.plugin.json`). Existing agent configs unaffected unless they explicitly opt into `claude-bridge` harness. Doctor contract added (`extensions/claude/doctor-contract-api.ts`).
- **Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** Yes (intentional). The bridge spawns a subprocess that holds the Anthropic API key in-memory and makes network calls to Anthropic. Subprocess lifecycle is owned by the harness, not user shell. The plugin SDK enabler changes (e.g. `command-registration.ts`) are additive and follow existing codex-extension precedent.
- **Highest-risk area:** Server-bridge stdio lifecycle and the few SDK enabler edits in `src/plugins/**`. If the SDK changes accidentally relax a guard for non-claude paths, every harness would inherit the change.
- **How is that risk mitigated?** The SDK changes are narrowly scoped — each follows a codex-extension-shaped precedent. Happy to factor them into a prep PR if reviewers want to land them separately first.

## Current review state

- **Next action:** Maintainer steer on scope: (a) accept as bundled extension as-is, (b) split SDK enablers into a prep PR first, or (c) request packaging as an external plugin.
- **Still waiting on:** Maintainer decision on the above. CI run on this push.
- **Bot/reviewer comments addressed:** None yet — opening draft.

---

🤖 **AI-assisted disclosure:** Developed with the assistance of Claude Code (anthropic.com/claude-code). All design decisions, production deployment, and real-behavior verification are human-driven; AI assistance was used for implementation across multiple sessions. Session transcripts available on request.
